channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: Book excerpt: The Game Narrative Kaleidoscope
item.content:encoded:
I've been writing games all my life—designing, too, but always writing. Text adventures when I was a teenager, experimental narrative games for Sony when I joined the industry, and finally at inkle, where every game we make is, at its core, some kind of story we want to tell. 

When I started out, this was fairly strange. The term "narrative designer" didn't exist (even though adventure games were being made by them), and writers were largely freelance, brought in at the last minute to fill in the boxes of the spreadsheets that ran the game. 

But the truth is the games industry has always been in the business of telling stories. In the early days, the games were so limited that most of the narrative was encoded into the box-art and the title screen; but over time, characters and worlds that were once painted graffiti-style on the sides of the Operation Wolf and Space Invaders arcade cabinets infiltrated the art and text of the games themselves. Modern-day Pacmen curse as they run away from ghosts, and cheer when they turn the tables with a cherry.

Related:Hades devs: accidentally inventing a subgenre was 'surreal'

This evolution has meant that, over the last twenty years, the number of writers working in the games industry has ballooned, fed by a talent pool self-trained on Twine stories and interactive fiction as much as on traditional novel, theater and screenwriting techniques. There are now so many games writers that we can disagree vigorously over issues of craft. We can love games that are despised by our peers. We can be overwhelmed with jealously and delight when something new and incredible is released out of nowhere to high acclaim, every couple of months.

Any lingering notion that there's one right way to approach games writing, narrative design, characterization or world-building has evaporated in the wild variety of a medium that combines learnings from everywhere else: film, comics, theater, novel-writing, poetry and lyric-writing, music, opera, dance, the visual arts, sculpting and more. 

So how do we talk about what we do, if there's no right way to do it? The original concept for this book comes from a friend, who—somewhat idly—floated to me the idea of a self-published anthology, by writers about writing. A book that could be affordably priced without asking too much unpaid labor from its contributors (writers do too much of that, anyway). I put out a speculative call for interest in the project, and received over 300 responses in two days. About half of those people have an article in the Kaleidoscope you hold in your hands: a lightly-curated mixture of seasoned experts who've worked on the biggest titles, adaptable freelancers who've done a bit of everything, indie writers pushing boundaries and budgets, juniors working in their first paid positions, and students of the form. 

Related:Battlefield 6's meta progression needed 'a little bit of a rework,' says producer

Everyone here was asked to say one thing, succinctly, and to focus on craft over critique. You'll find within inspiration, guidance, great ideas—and maybe a few false idols, too; ideas you dislike and want to pick apart. Every page might be the start of the something new. Don't read them in order. Open in the middle, at random. Let your eye drift until you catch a word you like, and start from there. 

Writers steal: writers fix: writers make old patterns work in new ways. This kaleidoscope contains jewels, gems, and colorful buttons reflected in different ways by a hundred different voices. What shape they'll resolve into is for you to discover.

- Jon Ingold, 2025

→ Now open this book to a random page, and begin!

Disclosure notice: Game Developer senior editor Bryant Francis contributed to the book.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/book-excerpt-the-game-narrative-kaleidoscope
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
